name: Stability Matrix for Linear Differential Equation
statement_text:
Let M (z) denote the stability matrix for a general linear
method. Then, for a linear differential equation (520a), (520b) holds with
z = hq.
proof_text:
Proof. For the special problem defined by f (y) = qy, the vector of stage
derivatives F is related to the vector of stage values Y by F = qY . Hence,
(500c) reduces to the form

                          Y             A    U         zY
                                  =                            .
                         y [n]          B    V       y [n−1]

It follows that Y = (I − zA)−1 U y [n−1] , and that

                     y [n] = zBY + V y [n−1] = M (z)y [n−1] .
proof_latex:
\begin{proof}
For the special problem defined by \( f(y) = qy \), the vector of stage derivatives \( F \) is related to the vector of stage values \( Y \) by \( F = qY \). Hence, \eqref{eq:500c} reduces to the form
\[
\begin{pmatrix} Y \\ y^{[n]} \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} A & U \\ B & V \end{pmatrix} \begin{pmatrix} zY \\ y^{[n-1]} \end{pmatrix}.
\]
It follows that \( Y = (I - zA)^{-1} U y^{[n-1]} \), and that
\[
y^{[n]} = zBY + V y^{[n-1]} = M(z) y^{[n-1]}.
\]

If the method is stable, in the sense of Section~51, then \( M(0) = V \) will be power-bounded. The idea now is to extend this to values of \( z \) in the complex plane where \( M(z) \) has bounded powers.

Just as for Runge--Kutta and linear multistep methods, associated with each method is a stability region. This, in turn, is related to the characteristic polynomial of \( M(z) \).
\end{proof}

variables.name: M(z)
variables.definition: stability matrix for a general linear method, defined as M(z) = V + zB(I - zA)^{-1}U

variables.name: z
variables.definition: z = hq, where h is the step size and q is the parameter from the linear test equation

variables.name: y^{[n-1]}
variables.definition: incoming vector of approximations at step n-1

variables.name: y^{[n]}
variables.definition: output vector of approximations at step n

variables.name: (A, U, B, V)
variables.definition: matrices defining the general linear method

preamble: The theorem concerns the stability analysis of general linear methods applied to the linear test equation y' = qy. The stability matrix M(z) is defined from the method's coefficients, and the theorem asserts that applying one step of the method yields the relation (520b) with z = hq.
